As to why people cling to these fallacies ... well I don't know, I guess it's for recognition. Now as a member of many fandoms I've found it easier to just ignore them, after all, it's just text or in the worst case scenario a pointless webcam rant on youtube.

text_enhanced:
The fact is, the tendency to claim that older generations of a franchise — like G1 and G2 My Little Pony or Mighty Morphin Power Rangers — are automatically superior to newer ones relies on what's called Appeal to tradition (a.k.a. Argumentum ad Antiquitatem) and Appeal to the masses (Argumentum ad populum, and its reversal). It is true that both of these are logical fallacies, and the reason is straightforward: neither the age of something nor its popularity actually tells us anything about its quality. They sustain no valid arguments unto a formal discussion because they bypass the substance of what makes something good or bad. Appealing to tradition is generally seen with statements such as "the old ways" — as if being older inherently makes something better, which simply doesn't follow logically.

And the appealing to the masses regards that a popular opinion is the truth (e.g. "My Little Pony sucks!"), while its reversal has the belief that a popular opinion is false (e.g. "You all say My Little Pony is good, therefore it sucks"). In both cases, the evidence shows that the reasoning fails because popularity — whether for or against — has no bearing on whether something is actually good or bad on its own merits.

As to why people cling to these fallacies ... well I don't know, I guess it's for recognition. Now as a member of many fandoms I've found it easier to just ignore them, after all, it's just text or in the worst case scenario a pointless webcam rant on youtube.

(Remember people, don't fall into the use of logical fallacies.)

The fact is, the potential for abuse with a Universal Basic Income would be enormous — and here's why that's inevitably true.

It would be like the old Roman grain allotments.

If the UBI is set at $500 a month, there's nothing stopping a politician from promising to expand it to $1000 to win votes. And once it's $1000, the next politician promises $2000. Then $3000. Then $5000. Then $10,000. Each step follows naturally and inevitably from the last, because each increase makes the next one easier to propose. Once you start down that path, there is no logical stopping point — the escalation becomes unstoppable.

And you *KNOW* that's exactly the chain of events that would unfold, because, like I said, there's perfect historical precedent. It is simply true that politicians will always outbid each other with the public's own money — it's the easiest bribe in the world, and once that door is opened, it can never be closed again.
